The trigger test is `testSyntaxError2()`, which tests the functionality of the `getLine()` method in the context of a syntax error. Root Cause: The root cause of the bug is that the `getLine()` method does not correctly handle the case where the line number is out of range or the line does not exist. Specifically, when the line number is greater than the number of lines in the file, the method returns `null` instead of throwing an exception or returning an error message.

Suggestion 1: Handle Out-of-Range Line Numbers
The `getLine()` method should be modified to throw an exception or return an error message when the line number is out of range. This can be achieved by adding a simple check at the beginning of the method:
```java
if (lineNumber < 1) {
    throw new IllegalArgumentException("Line number must be greater than 0");
}
```
Additionally, the method should also check if the line number is greater than the number of lines in the file and return an error message or throw an exception accordingly.

Suggestion 2: Improve Line Number Validation
The `getLine()` method should be modified to improve the validation of line numbers. Instead of relying on the `lastLine` and `lastOffset` variables, the method should iterate through the lines of the file and keep track of the current line number. This will ensure that the method correctly handles cases where the line number is out of range or the line does not exist.

Suggestion 3: Use a More Robust Line Parsing Algorithm
The `getLine()` method uses a simple line parsing algorithm that relies on the `indexOf()` method to find the next newline character. However, this algorithm can be fragile and may not work correctly in all cases. A more robust algorithm would be to use a `BufferedReader` to read the file line by line and keep track of the current line number. This will ensure that the method correctly handles cases where the line number is out of range or the line does not exist.
